questions.scenario:
Feature card:

```text
Name: downloadable receipt after checkout
Data: PDF receipt file
Write path: generated once after payment succeeds
Read path: signed-in customer downloads by order id
Bad outcome: receipt disappears or another customer can read it
```

What is the best first design?

options.text: Store the PDF in S3 and keep the S3 key plus ownership in RDS.
Store the PDF bytes directly in the order row because checkout already uses RDS.
Serve the PDF from `/tmp/receipts` on whichever task generated it.
Put the whole order record in S3 so receipt and order share one service.

questions.explanation: The receipt bytes are object-shaped, while ownership and authorization are record-shaped. S3 plus an RDS pointer keeps those promises separate and clear. A task-local path can disappear, and database blobs make RDS carry file delivery work.

questions.scenario:
Feature card:

```text
Name: checkout idempotency
Data: token sent by client
Read path: exact token lookup before payment work
Write path: create only if token is new
Bad outcome: one browser retry creates two orders
```

Which service shape is a reasonable fit?

options.text: DynamoDB, if the table is designed around exact-key lookup and conditional writes.
S3, because the token can be used as an object key and overwritten on every retry.
EFS, because multiple tasks need to see a shared file named after the token.
EBS, because idempotency is safest when stored on one worker's attached disk.

questions.explanation: The app already knows the key and needs an atomic create-if-missing check. That is a good DynamoDB-shaped problem. Shared files or object overwrites would force the app to rebuild safety behavior at the wrong layer.

questions.scenario:
Feature card:

```text
Name: report render scratch
Data: temporary chunks while creating a CSV
Read path: same process reads chunks during one job
Recovery: job can retry if chunks disappear before upload
Final result: upload to S3
```

What is the simplest acceptable storage promise for the chunks?

options.text: Local scratch can be fine if the chunks are truly disposable and the final result is copied to S3.
RDS is required because every file written by the backend needs transactions.
S3 object metadata is required because chunks are generated by a report job.
EFS is required even when only one process reads the chunks.

questions.explanation: Temporary storage is not bad when the team is honest that it can disappear. If the job can retry and the final output moves to S3, local scratch may be enough. The mistake is treating temporary chunks as durable results.

questions.scenario: A senior engineer reviews a new data feature before service selection. Which questions should they ask first?

options.text: What is the unit of data: row, object, item, mounted file, or scratch file?
How is it read back: by key, by SQL query, by object key, or by directory path?
What changes after creation, and what failure would hurt users or operators?
Which AWS service is cheapest today, before describing the data behavior?

questions.explanation: The useful review starts with data shape, read path, change pattern, and failure risk. Cost matters later, but choosing by price before the promise is clear can create expensive workarounds. The service name should come after the behavior.

questions.scenario:
Feature card:

```text
Name: vendor input processing
Data: large input files from a private transfer process
Read path: several workers need open, read, rename, and directory listing on the same tree
Path: /mnt/devpolaris-input/incoming
```

Which question best decides between EFS and S3 plus queue processing?

options.text: Do workers truly need shared filesystem semantics, or can each worker process a named object independently?
Can the files be encoded as JSON so they can become RDS rows?
Does the path contain enough slashes to count as an S3 folder?
Can one worker's EBS volume be mounted by every other worker automatically?

questions.explanation: EFS is useful when existing tools need a shared mounted directory. If the workflow can pass object keys through a queue, S3 may be simpler and more durable. The right choice depends on file semantics, not on the presence of a path string.
